creator: spork@inch.com
text:
Many packages/ports, especially OpenSSH, require a newer version of OpenSSL
than what was included with the base system.  In production machines,
it is generally not an option to upgrade the entire OS unless there is
a critical need to do so.

For example, FreeBSD 4.1 includes a version of OpenSSH with a remote root
exploit.  To upgrade to the most current version of OpenSSH, you must upgrade
to at least OpenSSL 0.9.6b (which also has security fixes).  Even if you
keep your ports tree up to date, openssh or openssh-portable will not build
without the latest OpenSSL.  If you attempt to build an up-to-date OpenSSL
from ports, you will get an error message stating that you cannot build it
because it is part of the base system.

At this point, you are forced to manually build OpenSSL and install it on
top of the base libs.  This is non-trivial for new users.

Fix: 

Either remove the openssl port (by definition, it should not work on any
4.x installs),

or,

Provide a binary package to update, much like the update tarballs to bring
parts of the base system up-to-date, or give the user the option to build 
and install the ports version on top of the system version if they so choose.
How-To-Repeat: 
cd /usr/ports/openssh
make

or 

cd /usr/ports/openssl
make
